perturbation.original_text: Both parties shall comply in all respects with applicable laws, regulations and court orders, including but not limited to laws and regulations on export control, in both parties' countries and other applicable countries.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties shall endeavor to comply with laws, regulations and court orders, including laws and regulations on export control, in both parties' countries and other applicable countries.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall comply in all respects' to 'shall endeavor to comply' weakens the obligation, making it less strict.  'Endeavor' implies an attempt, not a guarantee of compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR requires strict compliance with export control regulations. Using 'endeavor' suggests a lower standard of compliance, which is insufficient under EAR, where exporters are expected to know and adhere to all applicable regulations and licensing requirements.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall comply in all respects' to 'shall endeavor to comply' creates a legal contradiction because it weakens the legally required obligation to comply with export control regulations. The Law Explanation states that the EAR requires strict compliance. The changed text introduces ambiguity and a lower standard, suggesting an attempt rather than a guarantee. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly define 'strict compliance,' the very existence of regulations (as shown in both snippets referring to the EAR and its parts) implies a need for adherence, which is undermined by the 'endeavor' language.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Confidential Information shall be disclosed to only those people within its respective organizations or its agents, consultants, representatives or advisors who have a need to know the information and who are obligated under terms no less restrictive than those imposed by this Agreement on the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Confidential Information should ideally be disclosed to only those people within its respective organizations or its agents, consultants, representatives or advisors who have a need to know the information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'who are obligated under terms no less restrictive than those imposed by this Agreement on the Receiving Party' and replacing 'shall' with 'should ideally' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to ensure that recipients are bound by confidentiality agreements equivalent to this one. Without that obligation, the receiving party could disclose the information to people without confidentiality constraints, making it very vague.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets. By weakening the requirement to bind recipients with confidentiality agreements, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation of trade secrets, contradicting the intent of CUTSA to protect confidential information by making it more vulnerable to disclosure without legal recourse. This obligation now falls in a grey area.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to ensure recipients of confidential information are bound by confidentiality agreements, increasing the risk of misappropriation. The original text mandated that recipients be 'obligated under terms no less restrictive,' while the modified text changes 'shall' to 'should ideally' and removes the obligation, creating ambiguity. Snippet 1, from California Code CIV 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' as disclosure or use of a trade secret 'without express or implied consent by a person who...knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was...acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use.' The removed clause ensured this duty existed; its absence in the changed text makes it easier for misappropriation to occur without legal recourse, thus contradicting the intent to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information delivered to and/or in the possession of the Receiving Party shall be returned or delivered to the Disclosing Party, with all copies made thereof, in whatever form, if the Disclosing Party so requests.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information delivered to and/or in the possession of the Receiving Party will be considered for return to the Disclosing Party, with copies, upon request.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be returned or delivered' to 'will be considered for return' makes the return of confidential information discretionary rather than mandatory. Removing 'all copies made thereof, in whatever form' reduces the scope of what must be returned, and this is ambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: DTSA aims to protect trade secrets. By making the return of confidential information and all copies discretionary, the modified text increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation, weakening the protection afforded by DTSA. The obligation is now open to interpretation.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the return of all confidential information and copies, whereas the changed text makes the return of confidential information discretionary ('will be considered') and omits the mandatory return of 'all copies made thereof, in whatever form'. The Law Explanation states that this change increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation, weakening the protection afforded by DTSA. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, the explanation indicates this change contradicts the spirit of protecting trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions civil proceedings related to violations of trade secret protection, implying there are legal ramifications for not adequately protecting such information.

perturbation.original_text: The Disclosing Party does not make any representation or warranty, except as may be specifically provided in writing, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information, or as to its utility or suitability for any purpose of the Receiving Party and the Disclosing Party expressly disclaims any right of the Receiving Party to rely thereon, or any liability to the Receiving Party resulting from the use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Disclosing Party disclaims any liability to the Receiving Party resulting from the use of the Confidential Information unless gross negligence or willful misconduct is proven.
perturbation.explanation: By implying that liability exists in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct, the modified text introduces ambiguity about the Disclosing Party's duty of care. While a complete disclaimer might be permissible, this conditional disclaimer creates uncertainty and raises questions about the standard of care expected.
perturbation.law_explanation: While § 2316 pertains to disclaimers of warranty in sales contracts, it is relevant as the contract involves transfer of information. Including language about 'gross negligence' and 'willful misconduct' without clearly defining these terms creates ambiguity. It introduces an implied warranty about the information that is not explicitly disclaimed, creating confusion. The code states requirements for clear and conspicuous disclaimers to prevent misunderstanding. The 'gross negligence or willful misconduct' phrasing brings ambiguity. This might give the receiving party grounds for a case if the Disclosing party does not explicitly represent the Confidential information, but can be held liable by the new verbiage. This changes the scope and liability of the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2316 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2316. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this division on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2202) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2) Subject to subdivision (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that “There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof.” (3) Notwithstanding subdivision (2) (a) Unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like “as is,” “with all faults” or other language which in comm
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating the Disclosing Party disclaims liability except in cases of 'gross negligence or willful misconduct.' This creates a conditional disclaimer where the original text had a more complete disclaimer. The Law Explanation notes that including 'gross negligence' and 'willful misconduct' without clear definitions creates ambiguity and an implied warranty about the information, altering the scope of liability. Scraped Law Snippet 1, COM 2316, addresses disclaimers of warranty. While it focuses on sales, the principle of clear and conspicuous disclaimers is applicable. The 'gross negligence or willful misconduct' phrasing creates an ambiguity that weakens the disclaimer, potentially allowing the Receiving Party to claim liability where none was previously intended. This ambiguous introduction of liability where none existed constitutes a legal contradiction under the 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' definition. The modified text weakens the original intent of complete disclaimer, creating a situation where the receiving party could claim liability, undermining the original agreement and creating a situation that would likely fail legal scrutiny.
